perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees it will not use Confidential Information provided under this Agreement to guide or aid a search and evaluation for purposes of showing information, technical data, trade secrets, or know- how provided hereunder is in the public domain.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees it will use Confidential Information provided under this Agreement to guide or aid a search and evaluation for purposes of showing information, technical data, trade secrets, or know- how provided hereunder is in the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Receiving Party to use the Submitting Party's confidential information to prove it's already in the public domain. This contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, which is to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text directly contradicts trade secret law, specifically the prohibition of misappropriation of trade secrets for commercial gain. Using confidential information to prove it's in the public domain directly undermines the Submitting Party's competitive advantage and constitutes misappropriation under 18 U.S. Code § 1836.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The change directly contradicts the original intent of the NDA, which is to protect confidential information. The modified text allows the Receiving Party to use confidential information to prove it's in the public domain, directly undermining the Submitting Party's competitive advantage. The Law Explanation states that this constitutes misappropriation under 18 U.S. Code § 1836. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, pertains to civil proceedings related to violations of chapter 90, which covers protection of trade secrets. While the snippet provided focuses on civil actions and injunctive relief, the Law Explanation connects the change to misappropriation, a key element of trade secret violation. Therefore, enabling the use of confidential information to prove public domain status directly contradicts the purpose of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: The commitments in this Agreement shall terminate two (2) years following the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The commitments in this Agreement shall terminate one (1) month following the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one month may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information takes longer than that to develop or implement. This undermines the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: A one-month confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonably short to protect legitimate trade secrets, particularly in industries with long development cycles. This contradicts the principles outlined in Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition § 41, which requires a 'reasonable' period to prevent unfair competition through the exploitation of misappropriated information. The exact duration of what is reasonable depends on the specific trade secret and industry practices. A month is unlikely to suffice for many valuable trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: trade secret | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you trade secret The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) is a piece of legislation created by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) . The UTSA defines trade secrets and describes claims related to trade secrets. As of 2024, 48 states , the District of Columbia, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Puerto Rico, have adopted the UTSA. What is a Trade Secret UTSA's Definition The UTSA defines a " trade secret " as: Information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Non-UTSA Definition Prior to the development of the UTSA, improper use or disclosure of a trade secret was traditionally a common law tort . Sections 757 and 758 of the Restatement of Torts (1939) set forth the basic principles of trade secret law that were widely adopted by U.S. courts . In particular, §757, comment b, listed six factors to be considered in determining whether information constitutes a trade secret: The extent to which the information is known outside the claimant's business. The extent to which it is known by employees and others involved in the business. The extent of measures taken by the claimant to guard the secrecy of the information. The value of the information to the business and its competitors. The amount of effort or money expended by the business in developing the information. The ease or difficulty with which the information could be properly acquired or duplicated by others. Elements of a Trade Secret Claim There are typically three essential elements to a trade secret claim: The subject matter involved must qualify for trade secret protection (see "Scope" below for more on this). The holder of the subject matter must establish that reasonable precautions were taken to prevent disclosure of the subject matter. The trade secret holder must prove that the information was misappropriated or wrongfully taken. Use of a trade secret belonging to another does not always constitute misappropriation. There are two basic situations in which obtaining the use of a trade secret is illegal; where it is acquired through improper means, or where it involves a breach of confidence. Trade secrets may be obtained by lawful means such as independent discovery, reverse engineering, and inadvertent disclosure resulting from the trade secret holder's failure to take reasonable protective measures. The misappropriation of trade secrets is considered a form of unfair competition , and is discussed in the Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition . Misappropriation of Trade Secrets as a Crime In some circumstances, misappropriation of trade secrets is not only a tort ; it is a federal crime . Economic Espionage Act In 1996, Congress enacted the Economic Espionage Act ("EEA"), codified as 18 U.S.C. §§1831-1839 . In United States v. Sazonov , the defendant Sazonov has been charged with violating the EEA for "attempt[ing] to steal valuable proprietary computer code that took his employer years to develop . . . Sazonov was employed for thirteen years by Susquehanna International Group ("Susquehanna"), a financial services firm headquartered in Pennsylvania with offices in Manhattan." Scope of Subject Matter Customer Lists Customer lists and other lists related to customer business qualify for trade secret protection if the lists' information cannot be ascertained from other generally available sources. In Morlife Inc. v. Perry, 56 Cal. App. 4th 1514 (1997) , the California Court held that customer identities from an organization's list are protected as trade secrets if the identities are not generally kno
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality term from two years to one month. The LLM explanation and Perturbation Explanation argue that one month may be unreasonably short to protect legitimate trade secrets, contradicting the principle that the period should be 'reasonable' to prevent unfair competition through the exploitation of misappropriated information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines trade secrets and mentions that misappropriation of trade secrets is considered unfair competition. This suggests the change undermines the protection of trade secrets, potentially leading to unfair competition by allowing quicker exploitation. The original two-year term is reduced to one month and therefore significantly weakens the protection given to the original text. The one month period also seems unlikely to suffice, rendering the changed term unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the State of New York without regard to principles of conflict of laws of such state. The federal and state courts within the State of New York shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the State of California without regard to principles of conflict of laws of such state. The federal and state courts within the State of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change designates California law to govern the contract but designates Delaware courts as having exclusive jurisdiction. This is unusual and can create legal challenges, as California law will be interpreted by Delaware courts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally agree on a forum selection clause, the choice must still comport with due process. If none of the parties reside or conduct substantial business in Delaware, requiring adjudication there may violate the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. This is because the selected forum must have some minimum contacts with the parties or the subject matter of the suit. Randomly selecting a jurisdiction like Delaware without such connection could be deemed unfair and unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: personal jurisdiction | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you personal jurisdiction Overview Personal jurisdiction refers to the power that a court has to make a decision regarding the party being sued in a case. Before a court can exercise power over a party, the U.S. Constitution requires that the party has certain minimum contacts with the forum in which the court sits. International Shoe v Washington , 326 US 310 (1945). So if the plaintiff sues a defendant, that defendant can object to the suit by arguing that the court does not have personal jurisdiction over the defendant. Waiving Personal Jurisdiction Personal jurisdiction can generally be waived (contrast this with Subject Matter Jurisdiction , which cannot be waived), so if the party being sued appears in a court without objecting to the court's lack of personal jurisdiction over it, then the court will assume that the defendant is waiving any challenge to personal jurisdiction. See Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(2) . Obtaining Personal Jurisdiction Typically for a court to have personal jurisdiction over a defendant, the plaintiff needs to serve the defendant in the state in which the court sits, and the defendant needs to voluntarily appear in court . Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 4(k) describes whether a state's courts would have the authority to adjudicate a claim as it relates to personal jurisdiction. See also [wex:in personam] Keywords jurisdiction civil procedure Federal Rules of Civil Procedure SERVICE OF PROCESS subject matter jurisdiction Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS civil procedure wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract designates California law to govern the contract but specifies Delaware courts as having exclusive jurisdiction. The Law Explanation suggests this might violate the Due Process Clause if none of the parties reside or conduct substantial business in Delaware, because the chosen forum must have minimum contacts with the parties or the subject matter. Scraped Law Snippet 2, defining personal jurisdiction, supports this by stating that the U.S. Constitution requires the party being sued has certain minimum contacts with the forum in which the court sits, as per International Shoe v Washington. By selecting Delaware without such contacts, the modified contract introduces a legal contradiction because it could be deemed unfair and unenforceable, challenging the court's personal jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: NO materials or documents which have been furnished by Submitting Party to Receiving Party will be returned, but instead will be disposed of in an appropriate manner to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: All materials or documents which have been furnished by Submitting Party to Receiving Party will become the sole property of the Receiving Party and will not be returned.
perturbation.explanation: This clause states that all materials become the property of the Receiving Party, which could include documents containing trade secrets or proprietary information. This contradicts the NDA's purpose and could lead to misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Law - Duty to Maintain Secrecy
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires that a trade secret holder take reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. By stipulating that all materials provided become the Receiving Party's property and are not returned, the agreement effectively eliminates any ongoing obligation to protect the confidentiality of the Submitting Party's trade secrets, potentially leading to their disclosure or misuse without recourse. This contradicts the fundamental duty to maintain secrecy required by the UTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text stipulates that all materials become the property of the receiving party, negating the submitting party's ability to maintain secrecy, a key requirement for trade secret protection under UTSA. The original text ensures confidential disposal, while the changed text eliminates any obligation to protect confidentiality. Snippet 1 (CIV 3426.2) allows for injunctions against misappropriation. By transferring ownership, the agreement weakens the submitting party's ability to seek such an injunction or compel protective acts, thereby directly contradicting the UTSA's intent to protect trade secrets.
